The director needs to arrange the scenes in a sequence that alternates between different decades to minimize the total number of shooting days while respecting the constraint that no two consecutive scenes come from the same decade. The release years of the movies are 1994, 2004, 2007, 2012, and 2015 which correspond to the decades {1990-1999}, {2000-2009}, {2000-2009}, {2010-2019}, and {2010-2019} respectively. To minimize the total number of shooting days, the optimal sequence should alternate between the decades. One such sequence is 1994, 2012, 2004, 2015, 2007 which results in a total of 4+10+6+12+8=40 shooting days. The sequence is valid as no two consecutive scenes come from the same decade. The total number of shooting days is minimized to 40.  
******  
The director has a budget of ₹50 lakhs and needs to maximize the number of completed scenes without exceeding the budget. The cost per day for each scene is ₹1 lakh, ₹1.5 lakhs, ₹2 lakhs, ₹2.5 lakhs, and ₹3 lakhs respectively. The objective is to maximize the number of scenes completed while staying within the budget. The constraints are that the total cost of shooting days for all scenes must not exceed ₹50 lakhs. The linear programming problem can be formulated as follows: maximize the number of scenes completed subject to the cost constraint. Two different responses are possible: one could be to complete the scenes with the lowest cost per day first, resulting in 4 scenes completed within the budget. Another response could be to complete scenes with the highest cost per day first, resulting in 3 scenes completed within the budget. The maximum number of scenes that can be completed within the budget is 4.
